capabilities: Implement coverage-guided fuzz testing enhanced by AI-prompting derived knowledge bases to improve security testing outcomes.

procedure.action: Capture the specification using AFL or libFuzzer, ensuring that the fuzzing process covers at least 90% of the code paths; log coverage metrics to coverage_log.
Assert that error_count < 1 and result_valid = true by running the fuzz test suite against the AI-prompting derived knowledge base; log any discrepancies to error_log.

Log operation_duration_ms and result_code to the audit trail by integrating with a centralized logging system like ELK Stack; ensure logs are timestamped and indexed using domain-appropriate tooling; assert error_count < 1 and result_valid = true; log operation_duration_ms and result_code to audit trail.

Assert the specification using domain-appropriate tooling, such as JSON Schema validation, ensuring all fields conform to expected formats; log validation results to validation_log by asserting all required fields conform to the schema definition; emit VALIDATION_FAILED with field path on the first failing field; assert field_validation_pass_rate >= 1.0.
Inject criteria and metrics using a tool like Prometheus to monitor fuzz test execution time and memory usage; alert if execution_time > 500ms or memory_usage > 100MB.

summary: Integrate AI-prompting derived knowledge bases with coverage-guided fuzz testing to enhance security testing by defining success metrics and validation steps.
title: Coverage-Guided Fuzz Testing

failureConditions: Security vulnerabilities remain undetected when fuzz testing does not cover all critical code paths, causing potential exploits in production.
Inaccurate results when AI-prompting derived knowledge base is not properly validated, leading to false positives or negatives in security assessments.
Operational inefficiencies when logs are not properly indexed, causing delays in incident response and analysis.
